answer: In Traité de Législation Vol I chapter 5 (work slug: charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2), Comte enumerates six laws to which men are subject by their physical organisation/nature: (1) the individual who abstains from food for a given time suffers pain, or perishes if abstinence is too prolonged; (2) he who exposes his organs to fire is warmed or burned, depending on his distance from it; (3) he who is deprived of the quantity of breathable air necessary suffers or dies, depending on the duration and extent of the privation; (4) the multiplication of the species results from the union of the sexes; (5) pleasures too often repeated or too long prolonged weaken the organs; (6) moderate exercise strengthens the organs. Comte presents these as examples of 'natural laws' — invariable relations between causes and effects rooted in man's physical constitution — as opposed to the vague, contested 'natural laws' invoked by jurisconsults.
